one_line_takeaway: T2VShield is a training-free, model-agnostic input-rewriting + multi-scale output-screening wrapper that roughly halves-to-thirds jailbreak success on both open- and closed-source text-to-video models without hurting clean-video quality, though it relies heavily on LLM-based judging and still leaves ~12-22% residual ASR.
key_finding: T2VShield cuts jailbreak success sharply while preserving generation quality on clean prompts. On Open-Sora / T2VSafetyBench it reduces ASR from 52.9% (undefended) to 17.2%, GPT-4o score 50.7 -> 15.64, and Human 56.0% -> 19.6%, while clean-input CLIP similarity (0.163 vs 0.158), Temporal Consistency (0.989 vs 0.972) and FVD (634.5 vs 639.1) stay essentially unchanged. In contrast, in-model defenses that also cut ASR (Adversarial Training, Unlearning) badly degrade quality (FVD 747.1 and 919.4 on Open-Sora; up to 1268/1469 on CogVideoX) and cost >40,000s of training. Ablation shows all three modules contribute: LLaVA-only 51.1% ASR -> +Multiscope 42.2% -> +RiskTraceCoT 44.7% -> +RiskTraceCoT+PosNegRAG 29.7% -> full 22.4% (on Open-Sora). The defense generalizes to real-world SafeWatch prompts (CogVideoX 47.8%->12.3%, Open-Sora 49.2%->16.8%) and to closed-source models (Pika 31.3%->14.7%, Luma 28.7%->14.2%, Kling 75.5%->9.1%), and disrupts cross-modal audio-visual attacks (alignment accuracy 89.7%/81.2% -> 43.5%/50.1%).
core_contribution: T2VShield, a model-agnostic, plug-and-play jailbreak-defense framework for text-to-video (T2V) models that operates purely at the input and output stages, requiring no access to model weights or internal representations so it works on both open- and closed-source systems. Its own novel components are: (1) RiskTrace CoT, a four-stage chain-of-thought prompt-rewriting pipeline (risk comprehension -> risk-element identification -> directed rewriting -> safety self-verification) driven by a multimodal LLM; (2) PosNegRAG, a graph-based retrieval-augmented rewriting module that builds a multimodal similarity graph over positive/negative example pairs (intra-class edges tau_pos=0.7, inter-class edges tau_neg=0.3, text/image weight alpha=0.7) to inject concrete contrastive examples and reduce the rewriter's dependence on model capability; and (3) Multi-scope Output Detection, which slices each video at three temporal granularities (global / ~15-frame mesoscale / ~5-frame fine-grained) and fuses an image NSFW detector with BLIP-2 captioning plus a text-ambiguity/content classifier, flagging the whole video unsafe if any segment or modality trips (local-to-global subsumption). The paper also frames a three-stage (input/model/output) taxonomy of T2V defenses and empirically benchmarks nine existing strategies to motivate the design.
threat_model: Black-box jailbreak attacks on T2V generation (the paper's own Sec. 3). The attacker has no knowledge of the victim model's architecture, parameters, or filtering mechanisms (no gradients/weights), but has unrestricted ability to craft and iteratively refine input text prompts and can observe generated video outputs. The attacker's goal is formalized as evading input censorship (stealth constraint D(x_hat) <= tau_D) while inducing at least one generated frame v_t whose violation risk R(v_t) >= tau_R. T2VShield is the defender, sitting in front of and after the T2V model at the input (prompt rewriting) and output (video screening) stages without modifying the model.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) evaluation limited to two jailbreak datasets (T2VSafetyBench, SafeWatch), which may not cover all attack techniques or real-world risks; (2) for ethics/legal reasons certain highly sensitive prompts (e.g., specific political figures) are excluded from rewriting/detection, making results somewhat conservative; (3) the defense is visual-modality-only and does not yet integrate audio or other modalities. Reviewer-observed: safety is measured almost entirely by GPT-4o (which is also a component of the pipeline and a baseline) plus small human studies, risking judge/optimization circularity; residual ASR of ~12-22% still leaves substantial leakage; T2VShield adds meaningful inference latency (1700.8s per 100 samples vs 0.1s for keyword filtering); and the paper itself notes low-resolution generation gives attackers a cheaper optimization loop. There is also a naming inconsistency between the closed-source models claimed (Sora) and those actually tabulated (Pika/Luma/Kling).

datasets_used: T2VSafetyBench (14 high-risk categories, 20 prompts/category = 280 adversarial cases; primary jailbreak benchmark)
SafeWatch (50 sampled real-world harmful text descriptions; generalization test)
MSVD (300 clean text prompts; benign generation-quality test)
OpenVid-1M (300 clean prompt-video pairs; used only to build the adversarial-training/unlearning baselines)
SafeWatch pool for PosNegRAG (143 clean/positive + 717 harmful/negative video-text pairs)
quantitative_results: Open-Sora clean quality preserved: CLIP 0.158->0.163, Temporal Consistency 0.972->0.989, FVD 639.1->634.5

CogVideoX / T2VSafetyBench baselines (Tab 2): undefended ASR 55.6%; best input baseline Keyword Detection 44.1%; Model Checking 47.2%; Adversarial Training 0.0% but FVD 1268.0, Unlearning 0.1% but FVD 1469.5

Efficiency (CogVideoX, per 100 samples): T2VShield ASR 22.4%, 0s training + 1700.8s inference; Adversarial Training 0.0% ASR but 43,866.2s; Unlearning 0.1% but 41,366.1s; Keyword Detection 44.1% at 0.1s
Component ablation (Open-Sora): LLaVA 51.1% -> +Multiscope 42.2% -> +RiskTraceCoT 44.7% -> +RiskTraceCoT+PosNegRAG 29.7% -> full T2VShield 22.4% ASR
RAG ablation (Open-Sora): NegPosRetri 31.4%, NegRAG 28.2%, NegPosRAG (proposed) 26.3% ASR
Rewriter-model effect: Qwen-2B ASR 33.6%, GPT-4o 17.8%, DeepSeek-VL 19.3%
Audio-visual study (Tab 7): alignment accuracy Binary 89.7%->43.5%, Multi-choice 81.2%->50.1%; T2A match 45.3%->27.7%
Resolution robustness (Tab 10): T2VShield ASR ~16.8-17.7% across 144/240/360/480 vs undefended ~52-53%
Headline claims: reduces jailbreak success by up to 35% vs SOTA baselines; average ~33% reduction on commercial models to ASR <= 15%
baselines_compared: Input-level: Keyword Detection, Implicit Meaning Analysis (Toxic-BERT), Sensitive Word Segmentation
Model-level: Adversarial Training, Unlearning/Forgetting Defense, Training-free Defense (SAFREE)
Output-level: Visual Classification (MobileNetV2 NSFW), Video Semantic Detection (BLIP-2 + MA-LMM + BART-large-MNLI), GPT-4o Model Checking
